Something that many people refuse to acknowledge or overlook is that even this technology (renewables) is based on fossil fuels, and we are actually engaging in rematerialization, just in a different way.
In other words, there are no “renewables”; strictly speaking, there is such a thing as “rebuildables.” The sun is renewable, but not the technology we use to “capture” and utilize the energy. This
means that the entire infrastructure consists of physical materials (cables, solar panels, plastic, chips, aluminum, copper, cobalt, steel, concrete, glass, etc.). Everything also has to be built, maintained, repaired, replaced, disposed of, transported, etc. Machines used to extract rare earth elements, for example, require oil. Tankers used to transport everything do as well. Airplanes will, if
at all, only be able to be electrified in the distant future. Nevertheless, they must be built from real materials, and we also need airports, which in turn require materials, etc. It is a complex cycle that has developed over decades. Without oil or oil byproducts, there would be no plastic, concrete, steel, or even artificial fertilizers, for example. But these are the pillars of modern
civilization. Also important: half the world would like to reach the standard of the so-called West, which also means vast amounts of resources.

Another point is that we focus solely on the issue of “climate change.” As a result, other issues—such as soil erosion, ocean acidification, “forever chemicals,” and the destruction of ecosystems and habitats—are downplayed. Even without climate change, we need air, water, fertile soil, plants, animals, materials, and so on. Renewable energy does not solve these problems. Just so there’s no misunderstanding—I’m not an opponent; quite the opposite—but I simply want to highlight the complexity of the issue. I can recommend books by Vaclav Smil—'How the World Really Works' or 'Numbers Don’t Lie' and Ed Conway’s 'Material World: How Six Raw Materials Shape the History of Humanity', as well as podcasts like 'The Great Simplification' by Nate Hagens. Renewables can power a modern civilization, but not this one.

22:40 This is a somewhat misleading statistic. It looks at input, not output. When fossil fuels are used to generate electricity or move objects, the majority of the energy is lost to heat. This is not the case with renewables and EVs. So a wind turbine could give 1 MWh of electricity. A coal plant would burn 2.5 MWh to get 1 MWh but from input POV, coal is 71% of the total energy supply.
